titleStmt.title: Saudi Arabia 2022 – Speaker 14 – Childhood; Women’s Work
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Saudi Arabia Fieldwork Campaign Gunda Kinzl 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: Do you know the clover?
They take its flowers and tie them.
I will tell it to you in our dialect, they chop up its flowers.
and they gather them.
and they lay them on a big mat.
and they take a special type of stone.
it becomes polished.
and big, like in the shape of a bowl (kōwb is a rašīdi way, should be kūb).
but even (shaped), and it becomes neat (polished).
We used to make it as well, I remembered.
You are bringing back memories.
We would sit down with our mother and hit it.
We hit and hit until the flower is crushed and the seed would fall down.
and she would wait until a strong wind would come.
and when one day a strong wind comes, she would take all (the seeds) that she had gathered.
and she would go and spread it a second time.
and she would grab it like this and do like this.
this is all seeds and flowers.
grinded leaves, and she would do as such.
the seeds, she would do like this (mimics the sound of the crushed seed being blown away by the wind).
and the wind would blow the leaves and shakes the seeds down and sieves them down.
and that's it, # the seeds would become pure.
and the remaining, that you wouldn't want, that's it, the wind would have blown it here.
and they would take this, they would sell it by the kilo.
now that's it, it is the season...
The seed of the clover.
Yes, and they buy it.
and this was one of the jobs that the women would work in.
and in leather, they would work in this job and they would sell it.
((?? Until fire, or maybe a soft h in nahār which becomes nār, thereby meaning in the daytime))
And the children as well? Were the children with them (the mothers)?
not the children, the older children would work with them, sometimes those who did not have (children), no one would work with them.
It was a source, a source of income.
Especially those who have field, those who do not have they don't, they can't profit.
Before, it was like this as well. Before the girls and boys would help in the fields.
Even the workers were few
They weren't available. And they would, yes, they would cut off the weeds.
We would clear the weeds for a period of time, I remember.
And others as well.
Those who had their own fields they (unfinished word??) the girls and the boys, it was normal.
Those who had their fields far away, they would go every afternoon and pick out the clovers.
Their sheep would clear out (the clovers) every day. for example.
Allah, and pray upon Mohammed (often used when train of thought is lost)
Where are we?
The clovers! They would sell it every year, that's it the people, they would # and plough the land and plant new clovers
That's it, they would go and search
They would go find who has clovers and look for it and buy it
It was expensive, it was rare, the women who worked in this kind of job were few
Who has a field or something like this and the women who would be interested in this type of work?
And the women would raise animals, they raised them.
and they would raise the chickens and they were interested in this work beyond the villages.
When they came from the villages they would engage in the raising of chickens and rabbits.
Yes, as well.
Yes
The rabbits were for food?
yes they would be interested, they were interested in this work and they would slaughter, and they used to...
Is it the women or the men who would slaughter the rabbits? Or it didn't really matter?
The women did not slaughter but only raise.
for example for us, my older brothers would slaughter, it was like that.
It's possible, but they only used to, the most important of them would raise them.
In the houses.
Sections for the sheep, sections for the chickens, sections for the rabbits.
This was the time, you know? when we were
We were at the ages of fifteen, sixteen, seventeen, it was like this.
When we left for university, that's it, it was over, we no longer saw this, my mother occupied herself with the sheep.
and others around, everybody around her became like this, you understand?
Afterwards, they used to make the gbaʕa
What is a gbaʕa?
Yes they used to, now we remember, me and my sisters remember it
the gbaʕa is not a shawl, now the misfaʕ is like this.
this, by the way, has changed, this is one of the things that changed.
it was, it was like, not like your hiǧāb.
they used to sew it in this way, you would put in your face, and that's it, it would be like that.
It comes as a complete triangle, it does not sit like this, in one piece like us, do you notice?
And like this and that's it.
One piece and you do like so and so and that's it and we wear the niqāb.
They wouldn't.
Even at the..
It would go down to the shoulders or?
Right it's one piece, it's impossible for me to find it for you on the internet.
Qubuʕ and burka, let me write it
an old burka, it's impossible for me to find the old one
It's impossible to describe how it used to look to you
Impossible
Ah by God, Kirdishyan
We are interested (??), my children have cultural studies of the Turkish Ottoman period.
Do you know it?
Do you know about the Ottoman period?
The Ottomans, do you know about them? The Ottoman wars?
When you follow old movies, that's enough to know about the period of the Ottomans even up to the modern period, that's it (??)
